evals.prompt: Scaffold a new MIF ontology for a customer-support domain. Use the skill's scaffold_ontology.sh script to generate the YAML skeleton: run `bash $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/.claude/skills/ontology-manager/scripts/scaffold_ontology.sh customer-support 0.1.0 --extends mif-base > customer-support.ontology.yaml`. Then validate the generated file using `bash $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/.claude/skills/ontology-manager/scripts/validate_ontology.sh customer-support.ontology.yaml > validate-output.txt 2>&1`. Report the validation result.
evals.expected_output: customer-support.ontology.yaml is created with ontology.id=customer-support, ontology.version=0.1.0, the mif-base extends entry, and the cognitive triad namespaces. validate-output.txt ends with 'Status: VALID' (0 errors).

deterministic_checks.description: scaffold produced the ontology YAML file
ontology.id is set to customer-support
ontology.version is set to 0.1.0
the extends block lists mif-base as a parent
the cognitive triad _semantic namespace is present
the cognitive triad _episodic namespace is present
the cognitive triad _procedural namespace is present
validation was run and output captured
the scaffolded ontology passes validation with zero errors

expectations: The scaffolded YAML has the full standard structure: namespaces, entity_types, traits, relationships, and discovery sections.
The extends block is rendered under the ontology metadata block, not at top-level.
validate-output.txt confirms 0 errors — the skeleton is schema-compliant out of the box.

evals.prompt:
Scaffold a standalone ontology (no extends) with id `lab-notebook` at version `0.2.0`, then add a new entity type to it using yq. Run:
1. `bash $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/.claude/skills/ontology-manager/scripts/scaffold_ontology.sh lab-notebook 0.2.0 > lab-notebook.ontology.yaml`
2. Add an entity type named `experiment` with base `episodic` and trait `timestamped`, with a required `title` field of type string:
   `yq -i '.entity_types += [{"name": "experiment", "description": "A lab experiment record", "base": "episodic", "traits": ["timestamped"], "schema": {"required": ["title"], "properties": {"title": {"type": "string", "description": "Experiment title"}}}}]' lab-notebook.ontology.yaml`
3. Validate: `bash $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/.claude/skills/ontology-manager/scripts/validate_ontology.sh lab-notebook.ontology.yaml > validate-lab.txt 2>&1`
4. Inspect the entities section as JSON: `bash $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/.claude/skills/ontology-manager/scripts/inspect_ontology.sh lab-notebook.ontology.yaml --section entities --json > entities.json`
evals.expected_output: lab-notebook.ontology.yaml contains the experiment entity type with base=episodic, traits=[timestamped]. validate-lab.txt shows Status: VALID. entities.json is a valid JSON array containing an object with name=experiment.

deterministic_checks.description: scaffold produced the lab-notebook YAML
the new entity type name is present in the YAML
the experiment entity has episodic base type
ontology with added entity passes validation
inspect --json produced the entities JSON file
entities.json is valid JSON
entities array contains the experiment entity with correct base type

expectations: The experiment entity type appears in entity_types with name, base, traits, and schema.required all set correctly.
The trait reference (timestamped) resolves without error since it is defined in the same ontology scaffold.
entities.json from inspect --json lists both the scaffolded example-entity and the new experiment entity.

evals.prompt:
Validate an intentionally malformed ontology file to confirm the validator catches errors. First create a broken YAML file by running:
`printf 'ontology:\n  id: Bad_ID\n  version: not-semver\n' > broken.ontology.yaml`
Then run validation:
`bash $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/.claude/skills/ontology-manager/scripts/validate_ontology.sh broken.ontology.yaml > broken-validate.txt 2>&1; echo "EXIT:$?" >> broken-validate.txt`
Report what errors the validator detected.
evals.expected_output: broken-validate.txt contains two ERROR lines: one for the invalid ID format (Bad_ID has an underscore) and one for the invalid version (not-semver). The file ends with EXIT:1 (non-zero exit). Status line reads INVALID.

deterministic_checks.description: the malformed input file was created
validation output was captured
the bad-ID error is reported explicitly (ontology.id ... must match)
the bad-version error is reported explicitly (ontology.version ... is not valid semver)
validator reports INVALID status for the broken file
validator exits with code 1 for an invalid ontology
VALID is NOT present — the file is definitively invalid

expectations: The ERROR for the ID ('Bad_ID must match ^[a-z][a-z0-9-]*$') is reported explicitly.
The ERROR for the version ('not-semver is not valid semver') is reported explicitly.
The validator does not swallow both errors and report only the first one — both violations appear in the output.

evals.prompt:
Inspect the software-engineering example ontology that ships with the harness. Run the inspect_ontology.sh script against it:
1. Full summary: `bash $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/.claude/skills/ontology-manager/scripts/inspect_ontology.sh $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/packs/ontologies/software-engineering/software-engineering.ontology.yaml > inspect-summary.txt`
2. Entities section as JSON: `bash $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/.claude/skills/ontology-manager/scripts/inspect_ontology.sh $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/packs/ontologies/software-engineering/software-engineering.ontology.yaml --section entities --json > se-entities.json`
3. Relationships section as JSON: `bash $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/.claude/skills/ontology-manager/scripts/inspect_ontology.sh $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/packs/ontologies/software-engineering/software-engineering.ontology.yaml --section relationships --json > se-relationships.json`
Report entity count, relationship count, and a list of entity names.
evals.expected_output: inspect-summary.txt shows entity count >= 1 and relationship count >= 1. se-entities.json is a JSON array. se-relationships.json is a JSON object with at least one key. The ENTITY TYPES and RELATIONSHIPS sections appear in inspect-summary.txt.

deterministic_checks.description: full summary inspection output was captured
the summary includes the ENTITY TYPES section header
the summary includes the RELATIONSHIPS section header
the summary includes the NAMESPACES section header
entities section JSON was produced
se-entities.json is valid JSON
software-engineering ontology has at least 1 entity type
relationships section JSON was produced
se-relationships.json is valid JSON

expectations: inspect-summary.txt header line shows 'Ontology: software-engineering' with a version number.
se-entities.json lists at least the well-known entity types from the software-engineering ontology (e.g. service, repository, decision).
se-relationships.json is a named-key object (not an array) because relationships are a mapping in the YAML.

evals.prompt:
Convert the scaffolded biology-research-lab ontology to JSON, then to JSON-LD. Run:
1. `bash $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/.claude/skills/ontology-manager/scripts/convert_format.sh yaml2json $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/packs/ontologies/biology-research-lab/biology-research-lab.ontology.yaml biology-research-lab.ontology.json > convert-json.txt 2>&1`
2. `bash $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/.claude/skills/ontology-manager/scripts/convert_format.sh yaml2jsonld $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/packs/ontologies/biology-research-lab/biology-research-lab.ontology.yaml biology-research-lab.ontology.jsonld > convert-jsonld.txt 2>&1`
Report the structure of both output files and confirm both contain the expected ontology data.
evals.expected_output: biology-research-lab.ontology.json is valid JSON with ontology.id=biology-research-lab. biology-research-lab.ontology.jsonld is valid JSON with @context and @type=mif:Ontology plus the ontology data embedded. Both convert-*.txt files report success.

deterministic_checks.description: yaml2json produced the JSON output file
the JSON output is valid JSON
ontology.id is biology-research-lab in the JSON output
entity_types key is present in the JSON output
yaml2jsonld produced the JSON-LD output file
the JSON-LD output is valid JSON
JSON-LD envelope has a @context key
JSON-LD @type is mif:Ontology
yaml2json conversion reported success
yaml2jsonld conversion reported success

expectations: The JSON-LD @context value is the mif-spec.dev canonical URL (https://mif-spec.dev/schema/ontology/ontology.context.jsonld).
The original YAML ontology data (namespaces, entity_types, relationships, etc.) is fully preserved in both output formats.
No PyPI tools are invoked — conversion uses only yq and jq.

evals.prompt:
Convert a JSON ontology back to YAML using json2yaml mode. First scaffold a new ontology and convert it to JSON:
1. `bash $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/.claude/skills/ontology-manager/scripts/scaffold_ontology.sh roundtrip-test 1.0.0 > roundtrip.ontology.yaml`
2. `bash $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/.claude/skills/ontology-manager/scripts/convert_format.sh yaml2json roundtrip.ontology.yaml roundtrip.ontology.json`
3. `bash $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/.claude/skills/ontology-manager/scripts/convert_format.sh json2yaml roundtrip.ontology.json roundtrip-back.ontology.yaml`
4. Validate the roundtripped YAML: `bash $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/.claude/skills/ontology-manager/scripts/validate_ontology.sh roundtrip-back.ontology.yaml > roundtrip-validate.txt 2>&1`
Confirm the roundtripped YAML still passes validation.
evals.expected_output: roundtrip.ontology.json is valid JSON. roundtrip-back.ontology.yaml is a YAML file containing ontology.id=roundtrip-test. roundtrip-validate.txt shows Status: VALID.

deterministic_checks.description: YAML to JSON conversion succeeded
roundtrip.ontology.json is valid JSON
the JSON intermediate has the correct ontology ID
JSON back to YAML conversion produced a file
roundtripped YAML retains the ontology ID
validation of roundtripped YAML was run
the roundtripped YAML passes validation — format is lossless for required fields

expectations: The json2yaml roundtrip does not lose ontology.id or ontology.version — they survive through JSON and back.
The roundtripped YAML passes validate_ontology.sh with 0 errors, confirming the convert_format.sh json2yaml mode produces schema-compliant output.
No PyPI tools are invoked — conversion uses only yq.

evals.prompt:
Add a custom trait and a discovery pattern to an ontology, then confirm they appear correctly in the inspect output. Run:
1. Scaffold: `bash $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/.claude/skills/ontology-manager/scripts/scaffold_ontology.sh knowledge-base 0.1.0 > knowledge-base.ontology.yaml`
2. Add a custom trait named `reviewed`:
   `yq -i '.traits.reviewed = {"description": "Marks content that has been reviewed", "fields": {"reviewed_by": {"type": "string", "description": "Reviewer name"}, "reviewed_at": {"type": "string", "format": "date-time", "description": "Review timestamp"}}}' knowledge-base.ontology.yaml`
3. Add a content discovery pattern:
   `yq -i '.discovery.content_patterns += [{"pattern": "\\bknowledge\\s+base\\b", "namespace": "_semantic/knowledge", "context": "knowledge management"}]' knowledge-base.ontology.yaml`
4. Validate: `bash $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/.claude/skills/ontology-manager/scripts/validate_ontology.sh knowledge-base.ontology.yaml > kb-validate.txt 2>&1`
5. Inspect traits as JSON: `bash $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/.claude/skills/ontology-manager/scripts/inspect_ontology.sh knowledge-base.ontology.yaml --section traits --json > kb-traits.json`
6. Inspect discovery as JSON: `bash $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/.claude/skills/ontology-manager/scripts/inspect_ontology.sh knowledge-base.ontology.yaml --section discovery --json > kb-discovery.json`
evals.expected_output: kb-validate.txt shows Status: VALID. kb-traits.json contains a reviewed trait with fields reviewed_by and reviewed_at. kb-discovery.json shows enabled=true and content_patterns containing the added regex pattern.

deterministic_checks.description: knowledge-base ontology was scaffolded
the custom reviewed trait was added to the YAML
ontology with added trait and discovery pattern passes validation
traits section JSON was produced by inspect
kb-traits.json is valid JSON
the reviewed trait key is present in the traits JSON
the reviewed_by field is present in the reviewed trait
discovery section JSON was produced by inspect
kb-discovery.json is valid JSON
discovery JSON has at least 1 content pattern (the one we added)

expectations: kb-traits.json shows both the scaffolded traits (timestamped, confidence) and the new reviewed trait — none are lost.
The discovery pattern regex (\bknowledge\s+base\b) is stored correctly in the YAML and survives the inspect round-trip into kb-discovery.json.
The validator's regex check passes for the new pattern — the Python re module can compile it without error.

evals.prompt:
Attempt to scaffold an ontology with an invalid ID to verify the scaffold script rejects it. Run:
`bash $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/.claude/skills/ontology-manager/scripts/scaffold_ontology.sh 'Invalid_ID' 0.1.0 > bad-scaffold.yaml 2>&1; echo "EXIT:$?" >> bad-scaffold.yaml`
Then try a valid scaffold to confirm the script works normally:
`bash $CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR/.claude/skills/ontology-manager/scripts/scaffold_ontology.sh valid-id 0.1.0 > good-scaffold.yaml`
Report what happened with the invalid ID attempt.
evals.expected_output: bad-scaffold.yaml contains an error message about the invalid ID and EXIT:1 (non-zero exit). good-scaffold.yaml is a valid scaffold with id: valid-id.

deterministic_checks.description: output file was created capturing the invalid scaffold attempt
scaffold exits with code 1 for invalid ID
scaffold did NOT emit YAML with the invalid ID
valid scaffold with correct ID was created
valid scaffold has the correct ontology ID

expectations: The error message in bad-scaffold.yaml references the ID format constraint (^[a-z][a-z0-9-]*$) or says the ID is invalid.
No partial YAML skeleton is emitted before the error — the script fails cleanly without producing a corrupt YAML file.
good-scaffold.yaml passes a mental validation check: it has ontology, namespaces, entity_types, traits, relationships, and discovery sections.
